coredata.dc:title: Carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) modified ms/tz-BiVO4 heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic performance for water purification
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanan
Liu, Chang
Shi, Chunli
Sun, Wei
Lin, Xue
Shi, Weilong
Hong, Yuanzhi
coredata.dc:description: In this article, two carbon-based quantum dots, carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), were modified with BiVO4 heterojunctions to improve photocatalytic performance. When CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 or NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 is used as a photocatalyst for Rhodamine B (Rh B) photodegradation, the activity can be significantly improved compared with pure ms-BiVO4 and ms/tz-BiVO4. The ms/tz-BiVO4 heterojunction can valid speed-up the photoinduced charge separation and inhibition of interfacial charge recombination. In addition, quantum dots on BiVO4 heterojunction are able to act as an effective concerted catalyst to enhance the light harvesting efficiency and be used as the reaction sites, and thus cause the improved activity for Rh B photodegradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heterojunction
BiVO4
Carbon nitride quantum dots
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the founding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006057 ), " Doctor of Mass Entrepreneurship and Innovation " Project in Jiangsu Province , Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) ( 1062931806 ), Henan Postdoctoral Foundation ( 202003013 ), The Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province ( JJKH20200039KJ ), The Science and Technology Research Project of Jilin City ( 20190104120 ), The Project of Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan ( 20200301046RQ ), and The Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province ( YDZJ202101ZYTS070 ).

The authors would like to acknowledge the founding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006057), ?Doctor of Mass Entrepreneurship and Innovation? Project in Jiangsu Province, Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) (1062931806), Henan Postdoctoral Foundation (202003013), The Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province (JJKH20200039KJ), The Science and Technology Research Project of Jilin City (20190104120), The Project of Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan (20200301046RQ), and The Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province (YDZJ202101ZYTS070).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images for CNQDs, (b) PL spectra for CNQDs and bulk g-C3N4. (c) HRTEM images for NCQDs. (d) PL spectrum for NCQDs.
SEM images of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4.
SEM image of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (a) and the corresponding elemental mappings of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (b-f).
SEM image of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (a) and the corresponding elemental mapping images of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (b-f).
TEM and HRTEM images for NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (a, b) and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (c, d).
(a) XRD patterns of as-prepared ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 samples, (b) FT-IR of as-prepared samples, (c-d) XPS spectra of C 1s and N1s.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of pure ms-BiVO4, ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4, (b) Optical band gaps of ms-BiVO4 and tz-BiVO4.
(a) PL spectra, (b) Transient photocurrent response curves, (c) EIS of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4.
(a) The Rh B degradation of different samples, (b) cycling test for the photocatalytic degradation of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4, NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and ms-BiVO4.
Mott-Schottky diagrams of ms-BiVO4 (a), tz-BiVO4 (b), NCQDs (c) and CNQDs (d), and the CB and VB potentials of the samples (e).
Effect of IPA, BQ and EDTA-2Na on Rh B degradation in CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (a) and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (b) ESR tests of·O2- and·OH in CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (c, d) and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 (e, f).
Possible photocatalytic mechanism for Rh B degradation over the CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbon-based quantum dots (QDs) modified ms/tz-BiVO4 heterojunction with enhanced photocatalytic performance for water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, 212003, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, two carbon-based quantum dots, carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), were modified with BiVO4 heterojunctions to improve photocatalytic performance. When CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 or NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 is used as a photocatalyst for Rhodamine B (Rh B) photodegradation, the activity can be significantly improved compared with pure ms-BiVO4 and ms/tz-BiVO4. The ms/tz-BiVO4 heterojunction can valid speed-up the photoinduced charge separation and inhibition of interfacial charge recombination. In addition, quantum dots on BiVO4 heterojunction are able to act as an effective concerted catalyst to enhance the light harvesting efficiency and be used as the reaction sites, and thus cause the improved activity for Rh B photodegradation.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth in industrialization and environmental pollution are considered to be significant issues in society nowadays [1–3] . In order to solve this problem, the use of semiconductor photocatalyst to utilize the inexhaustible solar energy has attracted broad attention [4,5]. Thus, it is a challenge for researchers to develop efficient photocatalysts [6–8]. At present, common semiconductor photocatalysts have been broadly studied including TiO2 [9], CdS [10], and CuFe2O4 [11], etc.

In the past two decades, BiVO4 as an effective visible light photocatalyst has been widely studied in the field of photocatalysis. BiVO4 has three crystal phases, monoclinic scheelite (ms-BiVO4), tetragonal scheelite (ts-BiVO4) and tetragonal zircon (tz-BiVO4) [12–14]. Among them, tz-BiVO4 shows very low photocatalytic performance due to its wide band gap (≈2.90 eV), while ms-BiVO4 displays the best photocatalytic performance due to its narrow band gap (≈2.40 eV) [15]. However, the application of BiVO4 photocatalyst is limited due to its poor charge transport and surface adsorption. In order to improve the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4, numerous preparation and modification methods have been invented including surface sensitization, chemical modification and coupling with other semiconductor materials [16–18].

In recent years, the formation of surface junction has been proven to be an available strategy to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency. Recently, several heterojunction photocatalysts have been designed [19–21]. For example, Patricia et al. synthesized a porous core-shell TiO2@LaFeO3 heterojunction photocatalyst by sol-gel synthesis. The system used LaFeO3 as the crystal core, surrounded by dense and mesoporous networks of anatase TiO2 crystals with a tight interface, and thus enhanced the contact between the two semiconductor phases. Under sunlight and visible light, it can efficiently degrade myclobutanil pesticide in water [22]. Tian et al. synthesized a series of BiVO4 photocatalysts by adjusting the pH and hydrothermal time to control the crystal phase of m-BiVO4/t-BiVO4 heterostructure. Due to the effective separation of electrons and holes, the photocatalytic activity of the heterojunction photocatalyst was greatly improved [23]. However, in the above studies, heterogeneous materials were synthesized under harsh conditions such as high temperature, high pressure and multi-step synthesis [24]. Therefore, a simple, energy-saving and environmentally friendly method for the preparation of heterogeneous semiconductor photocatalysts is an ideal choice [25]. In order to realize the long-term development of photocatalytic technology and in-depth understanding of photocatalytic mechanism, it is still necessary to find semiconductor materials more suitable to modify BiVO4. Building energy level matching heterojunction (such as MoS2/BiVO4, BiVO4/TiO2) can not only overcome the inherent defects of materials, but also can obvious speed up the separation of photo-induced charge carriers due to its different energy band structures [26–34].

Carbon based quantum dots, including graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs)， carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), are new type of zero-dimensional carbon based materials. Due to their high electrical conductivity, good water solubility, good optical and catalytic properties, they have recently attracted considerable attention [35–38]. CQDs have good adsorption properties due to their high specific surface area and porous structure. Moreover, CQDs can cut down the possibility of reorganization of photo-generated electrons by generating additional electrons, so as to improve the photocatalytic property [39,40]. NCQDs also shows outstanding photocatalytic property in photocatalysis field [41]. Graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a novel nonmetal semiconductor material, which has been extensively studied in photocatalytic applications due to its nontoxicity, good chemical stability and photocatalytic activity [42,43]. CNQDs have also become an attractive research hotspot due to the shorter transfer distance and good photocatalytic performance.

In this work, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite photocatalysts were prepared by modifying BiVO4 heterojunction with two carbon-based quantum dots CNQDs and NCQDs, separately. The degradation of Rh B by CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 was studied under visible light. And photocatalytic property strengthen mechanism of as-prepared CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 was also discussed in detail.

The ms/tz-BiVO4 was prepared by simple hydrothermal method. A certain amount of Bi(NO3)3 .5H2O was added to HNO3 (3 mL, 2 mol L−1) under stirring. Then 2 mmol of NH4VO3 was added to the above mixing liquor. And the pH of the mixing liquor was adjusted from 1 to 2 with NH3·H2O. After mixing the above mixed liquor evenly, the prepared solution was transferred to Teflon autoclave and hold for 24 h at the temperature 150 °C. The as-obtained product was washed alternately with ethanol and distilled water, and the final products were dried overnight at 70 °C.

CNQDs were prepared by a reported Wang’s method [44]. The CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite was fabricated by a simple precipitation method. 100 mg of synthetic ms/tz-BiVO4 was added into 50 mL distilled water, and then the as-prepared CNQDs was added. After ultrasonicated for 30 min, the suspension was vigorously stirred for 3 h. Finally, the obtained product was washed with distilled water and dried overnight at 70 °C.

NCQDs were obtained used a reported method [45]. The preparation of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 is similar to the above. 100 mg of synthetic ms/tz-BiVO4 was added into 50 mL distilled water, and then, the as-prepared NCQDs were slowly dropped to the liquor, followed by stirring for 2 h. Finally, the obtained product was washed with distilled water and dried overnight at 70 °C.

A serious of analyses were carried out on the synthesized samples. The detailed structure of the samples was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). The surface morphology of samples was observed by field emission scanning microscope (SEM, JSM-6510). The crystal structure of the sample was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/MAX-2500, Cu K a radiation, Rigaku). Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was detected by a photoluminescence detector (Horiba evolution) with wavelength of 325 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was studied by Nicolet NEXUS470 FTIR in the range of 4000–500 cm−1. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was tested by ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectrum was studied by ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu). The electrochemical properties of the samples were studied by electrochemical workstation (CHI660C). And electron spin resonance (ESR) was tested by electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, A300).

The photocatalytic degradation experiments of Rh B were used to evaluate the degradability of various synthesized catalysts. In 10 mg/L Rh B solution, 20 mg catalyst was added and the solution was stirred in the dark for 60 min to achieve adsorption desorption equilibrium. After that, the degradation performance was observed under visible light irradiation for 140 min. During the above reaction, a part of the suspension was centrifuged at 5000 rpm at intervals to collect the supernatant. The concentration of Rh B was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

As shown in Fig. 1, the HRTEM images and PL spectra of CNQDs and NCQDs were performed. Fig. 1(a) and (c) show the morphologies of CNQDs and NCQDs. It can be seen from the figures that CNQDs and NCQDs are uniformly dispersed in the solution, with an average diameter of about 2 nm, and there is no obvious agglomeration phenomenon. Fig. 1(b) and (d) display the optical properties of CNQDs and NCQDs by fluorescence spectra. It can be observed that CNQDs emission peak is about 410 nm, which is blue-shifted 50 nm compared with the g-C3N4, and the peak intensity of CNQDs is significantly reduced. In addition, the emission peak of NCQDs is around 450 nm, which is concordance with the previous reports [46]. These above results indicate the successful preparation of CNQDs and NCQDs.

In addition, the morphology of the as-prepared samples was researched by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the ms/tz-BiVO4 sample displays the leaf-like morphology with an average size of about several micrometers. After loading CNQDs or NCQDs, the leaf-like morphology was maintained, indicating that a small amount of CNQDs or NCQDs supported has no obvious influence on the morphology of ms/tz-BiVO4 (Fig. 2b and c).

Figs. 3 and 4 show the elemental mapping of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4. It is obvious that the distribution shape and position of Bi, V, O, C, and N are the same as that of SEM, and the dispersion is uniform. It proves that CNQDs or NCQDs is existed in CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 or NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4, respectively, which provides powerful testimony to the formation of heterojunctions.

The TEM images show the leaf-like morphology of the composite samples. Fig. 5(a) and (c) shows the leaf-like morphology of the as-prepared NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4. Fig. 5(b) shows the HRTEM image of the NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 sample. NCQDs spacing of the lattice fringes is determined to be 0.2 nm, which is corresponding to the lattice spacing of (100) crystal face in NCQDs. And the spacing of the lattice fringes at 0.481 nm and 0.312 nm correspond to the lattice spacing of (101) and (103) crystal face in tz-BiVO4 and ms-BiVO4, separately. Fig. 5(d) displays the HRTEM image of the CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 sample. CNQDs spacing of the lattice fringes is determined to be 0.336 nm, which corresponds to the lattice spacing of (002) crystal face in NCQDs. And the lattice fringes of tz-BiVO4 and ms-BiVO4 are 0.258 and 0.224 nm, which can be corresponds to the crystal face of tz-BiVO4 (220) and ms-BiVO4 (121), separately. The presence of ms-BiVO4 and tz-BiVO4 lattices can be clearly observed in the HRTEM image of the composite samples, which further confirmed the formation of heterojunction. The above results proved the formation of BiVO4 heterojunction and the successful loading of CNQDs and NCQDs.

The crystalline phases of samples with diffraction peak were measured using XRD. Obviously, all the peaks of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 samples were matched to tz-BiVO4 (PDF# 14-0688) and ms-BiVO4 (PDF# 14-0133). It shows that there are two crystal phases, tz-BiVO4 and ms-BiVO4 in all the composites, which further proves the successful fabrication of heterojunction. However, no phases of CNQDs and NCQDs were detected in the XRD patterns of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4, which may be due to the small size and the low amount of the quantum dots. Fig. 6(b) shows the FT-IR pattern for ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4. It can be observed that the characteristic peaks at 1200–1600 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of C═N and C─N, while the vibration peak at 807 cm−1 is considered to be the typical breathing mode of the tris-triazine units [47,48]. In ms/tz-BiVO4, the vibration bands located at 736 cm−1 belong to the asymmetric tensile vibration of VO4 2-. Apparently, we can see that the characteristic peaks of CNQDs and ms/tz-BiVO4 may be found in CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4, which suggests the existence of CNQDs in the composites. However, the absorption peaks of NCQDs are not obvious in the NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composites, which may be owing to the small content of NCQDs. XPS spectra of C1s of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 are shown in Fig. 6(c). The C1s peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to C─C, and 286.2 and 288.1 eV are ascribed to C─N and C═O, separately. In the N 1s spectrum of Fig. 6(d), the peak in 397.1 eV corresponds to triazine rings (C─N═C) and the other peak at 399.7 eV is attributed to the tertiary nitrogen [N-(C)3].

As shown in Fig. 7(a), UV–vis absorption band edges of the composites are displayed. Compared with the pure ms-BiVO4, ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composites shows obvious red shift in the absorption edges, and their absorption of visible light is enhanced. As we all know that the calculation formula of band gap energy is: αhʋ = A(hʋ - E
g ) n/2 (1), where A,α, h, υ, and E g are constant,absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, and band gap, where n depends on transition properties of semiconductors. For BiVO4, the value of n is 1. Furthermore, the E g presents in Fig. 7(b) displayed tz-BiVO4 and ms-BiVO4 are 2.83 and 2.34 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7, tz-BiVO4, ms-BiVO4, ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 samples are all yellow powders, and the color after loading quantum dots is darker than that of pure phase and ms/tz-BiVO4 heterojunction.

The specific surface area and pore size of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 were measured by N2-adsorption-desorption isotherm. As shown in Fig. S1, all the tested samples showed typical type IV isotherms. The specific surface areas of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 were 0.59803, 0.68769 and 0.67463 m2g−1, respectively. The illustration in Fig. S1 shows the pore size distribution of the samples. It can be observed that the samples have a large number of pore size distribution in the range of 1–10 nm. PL spectra of ms/tz-BiVO4, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 are shown in Fig. 8(a). BiVO4 shows a strong PL emission peak near 520 nm, which may be attributed to the recombination of electron hole pairs. The stronger the PL emission peak means the easier the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The emission peak intensities of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 were significantly lower than those of ms/tz-BiVO4 after the addition of CNQDs and NCQDs, respectively, indicating that the recombination of photogenerated e--h+ pairs decreased and the quantum dots were conducive to the separation of electron hole pairs. Fig. 8(b) shows the relationship between the photocurrent response of the synthesized sample and the on-off cycle of the irradiation signal. Obviously, among all the samples, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composites showed higher photocurrent intensity. The results show that CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composites have efficient separating for photogenerated carriers, indicating that CNQDs or NCQDs loading can effectively reduce the photogenerated e--h+ pairs recombination. This result is consistent with the PL. As shown in Fig. 8(c), Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is usually used as a means to study the charge transfer behavior and photo-generated charge efficient efficiency of synthesized catalysts. As we all know, in EIS spectrum, the arc radius represents the resistance of charge separation. The smaller the arc radius is, the higher the efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation is. Among them, the arc radius of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composites is significantly smaller than that of ms/tz-BiVO4. It shows that the loading of CNQDs or NCQDs can accelerate the interface electron transfer. The above results show that the introduction of CNQDs or NCQDs in composite can significantly improve the separation rate of photo-induced carrier, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

As exhibited in Fig. 9(a), the photocatalytic degradation process of the prepared photocatalysts was investigated with Rh B as a model pollutant. Firstly, before irradiated with visible light, the Rh B was reacted with catalyst for 60 min under dark condition to achieve sorption desorption equilibria. Obviously, the degradation effect of ms-BiVO4 on Rh B are not obvious within 140 min of visible light illumination. Compared with the ms-BiVO4, the photocatalytic property of the ms/tz-BiVO4 was enhanced as a result of the formation of heterogeneous junctions (88% degradation in 140 min). After loading CNQDs and NCQDs, the photocatalytic property of the ms/tz-BiVO4 sample was further enhanced, and the degradation rates of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite samples were about 90% and 98%, respectively.

Fig. S2 and S3 shows the ability of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 sample to degrade Rh B in different environments. Fig. S2a and S3a shows the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 at different Rh B concentrations. The degradation efficiency of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 decreased with the increase of Rh B concentration. It may be due to that the increase of pollutants occupied more active sites, resulting in the degradation efficiency decreased. In addition, with the increase of pollutant concentration, the opacity of High Concentration Dye increases, which affects the light utilization efficiency of photocatalyst. Fig. S2b and S3b shows the effect of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 catalyst dosage on Rh B degradation efficiency. When the dosage of catalyst was increased from 10 mg to 20 mg, the degradation efficiency was significantly improved. Because more catalysts provide a large number of electrons and holes, the photocatalytic performance is improved. However, when the dosage of photocatalyst was further increased to 30 mg, the degradation efficiency was not significantly improved, so the optimal dosage of photocatalyst was 20 mg. Fig. S2c and S3c shows the degradation efficiency of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 at different pH, respectively. The degradation effect of NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 was the best under neutral condition, and the inhibition effect was obvious under acidic and alkaline conditions. However, the inhibition effect of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 is obvious in alkaline condition, and it is better in acidic and neutral conditions.

Fig. 9(b) and (c) shows the stability of the photocatalysts. CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 were tested on Rh B for four cycles. After four cycles, it was found that the degradation rates of both photocatalysts have only slightly decreased, proving that both the CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 photocatalysts have good stability.

Fig. 10(a)–(d) display the Mott-Schottky diagrams of ms-BiVO4, tz-BiVO4, NCQDs and CNQDs. The Mott-Schottky curves of all samples show the positive slopes, which indicating that the samples are n-type semiconductors. It can be inferred from the X-axis intercept of the curve, the flat-band potentials of ms-BiVO4, tz-BiVO4, NCQDs and CNQDs are −0.11, 0.46, −1.15 and −0.81 eV, respectively. Generally, in n-type semiconductors, the conduction band (CB) potential is usually 0.1–0.3 eV lower than the flat band potential. Therefore, the CB potentials of ms-BiVO4, tz-BiVO4, NCQDs and CNQDs can be calculated to be 0.13, 0.7, −0.92 and −0.57 eV, respectively. According to the formula E VB = E CB + E g, the VB of ms-BiVO4, tz-BiVO4, NCQDs and CNQDs were calculated to be 2.47, 3.53, 2.26 and 2.16 eV, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 11(a) and (b), three sacrificial agents IPA, EDTA-2Na, and BQ were used as •OH, h+, and •O2 - scavengers, respectively. Obviously, for CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 photocatalyst, the photodegradation rate is markedly decreased in the existence of EDTA-2Na and BQ. Therefore, it can be improved that •OH and •O2 - play the key role in this photocatalysis process. For the NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 photocatalyst, the photodegradation rate was markedly reduced due to the existence of EDTA-2Na and BQ, and the photocatalytic activity was also relatively suppressed in the existence of IPA. The result show that •OH, •O2 - and h+ are the main active species. Using DMPO as a capture agent, ESR spin trapping experiments of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 were carried out. In the presence of photocatalysts, under the dark condition, no DMPO- •OH or DMPO-•O2 - signal peak was found, which proves that •OH/ •O2 - radicals would not be produced under dark conditions. The signal peaks of DMPO- •OH/•O2 - could be noticed obviously under four minutes visible-light illumination. These above results indicate that for CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite photocatalysts, •OH and •O2 - played an important role in the photocatalysis system.

According to the above discussion, the possible degradation mechanism of CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite photocatalysts is proposed ( Fig. 12). For CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 photocatalyst, under visible light irradiation, tz-BiVO4, ms-BiVO4 and CNQDs can be stimulate to produced e--h+ pairs. According to tz-BiVO4, ms-BiVO4 and CNQDs band structure, the e- from CNQDs migrated to the ms-BiVO4 CB, and then to the CB of tz-BiVO4, and is easy to be reacted with the oxygen in the solution to form •O2 -. Meanwhile, h+ from tz-BiVO4 migrated to ms-BiVO4 VB, and then to VB of ms-BiVO4, and reacted with H2O in the system to produced •OH. Both •O2 - and •OH are strong oxidizer agents, which are able to efficiently oxidate the organic pollutants. For NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite photocatalyst, ms-BiVO4, tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs can be stimulated to produce e--h+ pairs. At this point, e- on the CB of ms-BiVO4 can transfer to the CB of tz-BiVO4, and h+ on VB of tz-BiVO4 can transfer to VB of ms-BiVO4, and e- on ms-BiVO4 CB tends to compound with NCQDs h+. In addition, the CB border potentials of NCQDs is (−0.92 eV vs. NHE), which is more negative than that of O2/•O2 - (0.33 eV vs. NHE). Therefore, e- on NCQDs CB can react with O2 in the environment to generate •O2 -. And the E VB of tz-BiVO4 is 3.53 eV (vs. NHE), which is more positive than that of •OH/OH- (2.40 eV vs. NHE). Therefore, h+ on the VB of tz-BiVO4 can react with H2O to produce •OH. The •O2- and •OH can degrade Rh B. In addition, the h+ with strong oxidation has ability to directly degrade Rh B.

In this work, CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composite photocatalysts were prepared by loading two quantum dots CNQDs and NCQDs on BiVO4 heterojunction. The CNQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 and NCQDs-ms/tz-BiVO4 composites displayed excellent photocatalytic performance for Rh B degradation in visible light. Obviously, the key reason for the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency was due to the formation of heterojunction and the loading of quantum dots, which can enhance the light harvesting efficiency and promote the separation and migration of photoproduction carrier. This research provides a new theoretical guidance for photocatalytic degradation of pollutants.

Yanan Liu: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Chunli Shi: Validation, Methodology. Chang Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Wei Sun: Writing - review & editing. Xue Lin: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Weilong Shi: Formal analysis, Supervision. Yuanzhi Hong: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the founding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22006057), "Doctor of Mass Entrepreneurship and Innovation" Project in Jiangsu Province, Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) (1062931806), Henan Postdoctoral Foundation (202003013), The Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province (JJKH20200039KJ), The Science and Technology Research Project of Jilin City (20190104120), The Project of Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan (20200301046RQ), and The Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province (YDZJ202101ZYTS070).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.160437.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
